The golden era of the design community is over.

Thoughts about the history of the online design community and the rise of other communities.

1/ I miss the days of online design forums when conversations were purely about design.
2/ I got into design because of these phpBB/vBulletin forums. Designers were used to hanging out on many different platforms. Knowledge and content were distributed.
3/ Next @dribbble launched and quickly became very popular. I remember myself relentlessly scanning @Twitter for an invite.
4/ I loved these early days of dribbble. It was full of authenticity, full of inspiration. Alienation-free. It was my gate to the global design community.

5/ These were different times. There were several active and engaging communities.

Forrst by @kylebragger is a great example. Its immense growth got the company to be acquired 2 times in one year.
6/ DesignerNews was, and perhaps still serve as the go-to website for designers to share news and stay updated with the industry. However, in the past few years, it really faded.
7/ Other indie projects like @greatdiscontent, @wayswework, and @offscreen were also a huge source of inspiration for me. I wish similar publications existed today.
8/ The design community has been long-awaited for a savior. @figmadesign not only broke the market but took the community in an interesting direction, but it's still at its very beginning.
9/ I feel like @AREdotNA might revive the community with its user-owned social network. It reminds me of the early days of dribbble.
10/ Communities are great. I enjoy watching a thriving community like the developer's one. @github thrived the open-source culture. Other projects like @vercel and @GatsbyJS continue the tradition.
11/ Another community that's on the rise is the maker's one. @IndieHackers, @wip, and @makerlog are the proof that online communities can live on a platform of their own.
12/ We need more communities, not tools. There are so many new tools for designers but so fewer communities. Tools make our job easier. Communities make us better designers.

Transparency:

- When I hop on to calls, I often take notes in front of the client's eye right inside the artboard. We're all aware of what has been discussed and what's needs to be done.
Using @figmadesign hasn't just made my design process easier and efficient, it also improved the way I communicate with clients:

Transparency:

- When I hop on to calls, I often take notes in front of the client's eye right inside the artboard. We're all aware of what has been discussed and what's needs to be done.

- Having a specific and public link for each project enables clients to review work-in-progress whenever they feel like. There's no need to send back and forth old and new versions, just one source of truth.

Presenting:

When everyone on the other side is synced with the latest version, there's no need to share the screen. I simply go through the design like I'd do if we were in a physical conference room.

The @RoamResearch approach for product-market fit is really interesting. I see it as a new way to identify your user personality:

1. Find a niche that would really appreciate your unique solution. Get your product adopted in a suitable community.

2. Double down on that niche as a way to:

- Get instant feedback
- Build especially for your audience
- Fund your company in the early days

3. Expand to other markets.

If you were able to find the type of user who would use your product in one market, chances are high to find more in other markets.

The idea is to track journeys, whether it's a new habit or even the progress of a new project.

לפני שבועיים @OmryRozenfeld ואני הרמנו בוט פשוט שמאפשר ללמוד כל מיני נושאים באמצעות שיחה בטלגרם. ↓

איך זה עובד?

- שולחים לאדווארד (הבוט והקפטן) נושא שעליו רוצים ללמוד.
- מקבלים 5 פרקים בהתאם לנושא עם אפשרות להעמיק בתוכן בכל פרק.
- בסיום מקבלים קובץ PDF עם כל החומר ובנוסף גם תמונה מגניבה שמיוצרת מאחורי הקלעים. ↓

כדי לקבל את כל זה אנחנו עובדים עם כמה מודלים:

1. דה וינצ׳י שמתקשר עם המשתמש
2. דאלי לתמונות המהירות
3. מידג׳ורני לתמונות היפות

חוץ מזה יש אפליקציית ריאקט פשוטה בשביל הספרייה של הקורסים. ↓

את הבוט בנינו על גבי טלגרם - פלטפורמה מדהימה שלא נראה לנו שהרבה מודעים אליה:

- חוסך זמן ופיתוח
- ממשק נוח לעבוד איתו
- לא צריך לפתח יכולות זיהוי לאנשים

↓
את כל הקורסים שאנשים עושים דרך הבוט, אנחנו מאגדים לתוך ספרייה חופשית שבה אפשר לעיין ולהוריד כל קורס. ↓

מהר מאוד התחלנו לראות אנשים לומדים על כל מיני נושאים - ממדע ועד סדרות טלוויזיה. ↓ https://t.co/Ei5H2mSDIO

Over the last month, @OmryRozenfeld and I started experimenting with some of the latest AI models.

We're starting with a Telegram tutor bot that teaches any desired topic through a mini-course.

It's called Edward Teach Bot and we think it's super cool ↓

How does it work?

1. Send Edward (the bot and captain) a topic
2. Get a brief course overview with 5 chapters to dive in
3. Receive a PDF including all content + a beautiful cover image ↓

The models we make use of are:

1. Davinci to communicate with the user
2. DALL·E for in-chat images
3. Midjourney for a high-quality image ↓

Besides that, there's a super simple React app that takes all courses to a public library where users can browse and download other interesting courses.

Edward is built on top of Telegram, an amazing platform that we think is underrated – but that's for another time. ↓

In the beginning, the idea seemed kinda stupid.
We set up a simple landing page and started to post it on Reddit without any expectations.

However, we immediately started to get positive feedback, as more and more people engaged with Edward. ↓
From TV shows to science topics, the library got surprisingly big. ↓ https://t.co/OL7mzLxlWA

It's almost 2 weeks since we first start to promote it. So far we've got:

- 800+ sign ups
- 850+ courses have been taken

There is obviously plenty of work to do, but we think there's something here.

We'd like to know what you think!

https://t.co/st1ZrXRZCr

I had to think about this for a while but I've got a few thoughts and ideas on this.

The process of curation is essential for reducing a lot of noise: https://t.co/MonhjfXrTF
- Ever since I came across @AREdotNA, I've started to collect different things.  From random to design-related stuff. It kinda became my place to save internet bookmarks.

- I've always thought about it as a tool not only to dump my findings / visual bookmarks but also to get a glimpse into others' minds.

- Also I've had this idea of using its API to let people discover my stuff.

- As weird as it sounds – can I use it to open-source part of myself?

- I also think that @Twitter bookmarks is perhaps the most interesting place for discovering the internet. What if you could browse others' bookmark lists?
- If I consider my bookmarks as a goldmine for myself, wouldn't it be for others too?

The Zoom alternative you probably haven't heard of

@whereby , previously named https://t.co/51IAzdJjgd, is a simple video conferencing tool focusing on collaboration, launched in 2013. ↓

1/ Whenever I host a remote video call, I can already predict what would be the response of my invitee: “What's that tool we’re using?" That’s because I use @whereby  as my default video conferencing tool. https://t.co/AvbJPbXTS3

2/ Why meetings aren’t novel

As @hobdaydesign writes: Novelty distracts and is dangerous.

The fundamental issues of video calls have been ignored, perhaps deliberately, but as we’ve come to realize, people just don’t care about meetings as much as some wished. https://t.co/BxQy4nTY8N

3/ How might the meeting concept be improved instead of being forced to change?

That's exactly what I think @whereby has nailed. It just built something that works. A simple product that allows people to hop on a remote call effortlessly. That’s it. Nothing fancy. https://t.co/XKYGTdB6oc

4/ In the tech kingdom

As an app for professionals, it only makes sense that it would be focused on tools that connect people and teams around work interests. Therefore, most of their built-in tools and integrations revolve around the idea of making collaboration easier. https://t.co/w6WWSiuFrE

- How many times have you screen-shared a Miro or Trello board with a peer?

- Can you count how many times you’ve heard something like “Can you let me share my screen?”

- Do you remember where to do that all the time?

These are some of the hassles Whereby tackles.

- Enabling participants to share Miro and Trello boards (among other tools) directly into the meeting interface makes collaboration seamless and easy.

- Multiple screen sharing is available for the benevolent.

- You can set a customized room link,tring might get in your way. https://t.co/0sFmrN0tkc

5/ Outside the kingdom

The draw of Whereby is in how lightweight it is. And it feels its use case. Although it’s similar to other video conferencing tools for meetings in a professional context, it has a fast-casual and friendly atmosphere just by the way it's designed. https://t.co/EzeCaHa4F8

On top of that, Whereby Embedded, the second half of the platform enables hosts to grab a meeting room and embed it inside an external website. That way, customers of customers can experience the product without even being aware of it.

In addition, its HIPAA compliance serves as a great alternative for healthcare companies that need a high level of security.

When the host takes full govern of the meeting, the participant becomes more passive, perhaps relaxed. https://t.co/HhyjRQts00

6/ A short appendix on the dark side of simplicity

You try to be novel in the way you build, tirelessly think about how to solve customers’ real-life problems, and then with a click of a button, you can be at the stake. https://t.co/kCGKH0Ytxx

On niche product design

1/ I came to realize that the most inspiring, and authentic design on the internet today is coming from the most niched places. >>

2/ The tremendous acceleration of technology over the past decade has shaped the product design discipline.

However, once a culture becomes mature enough, it starts to develop its own bad habits. >>
3/ Popular products of tech giants as we all know not only dominate the entire web in many aspects, but they stand as a dogma in the field of product design, serving as trendsetters. >>

5/ Throughout the years, there have been many voices that criticized the current state of the product design community. Including myself: https://t.co/mn1B2UafIL

More to read:
- https://t.co/ykeesc2tSu
- https://t.co/orPqeIJLFi >>

6/ However, I've started to witness more and more products leaning into a different design direction, which I like to think of as 'niche product design'. These products are usually:

• Opinionated by design
• Being developed by individuals or a small team
• Targets a niche >>
7/ Reflecting on those thoughts, it very much boils down to growth. As companies grow, they gradually move from a state of 'fan-only' to a state of a product for everyone. >>

8/ "We lost our soul and growth was slow and I failed to raise a Series A investment round and Ebay." @pieratttt
https://t.co/vVyh0ZSo0U >>

9/ Growth is probably the most significant force that limits design at any startup that pursues scale. And while growth is the holy grail in this world, it has paradoxically become the Achilles heel of design. >>

10/ I try to nourish a little garden, filled with relevant references:

Are we in a new era of bookmarking apps?

Thinking about the heyday of bookmarking/save-for-later apps more than a decade ago:
- @Pocket
- @Pinterest
- @Delicious
- @Pinboard
- @AREdotNA

It's amazing that the listed above are either still running or have an online presence.

Despite the AI hype, it feels like we're in the middle of a "note-taking" wave of bookmarking products.

I see more and more products being built around curation/visual bookmarking:

@thecosmos is branded as the Pinterest alternative for creatives
@sanedotfyi looks like a visual knowledge graph

As an avid bookmark curator, I find this blooming really positive. There might be many more areas where this concept could work, which makes me also wonder whether Twitter/X will ever develop further its bookmarking feature.

What comes to your mind when you hear the word Telegram?

I feel like Telegram is a mystery. On the outside, it might be perceived as a platform designed for drug traffickers, crypto scammers, and sexual abusers. https://t.co/BsDgLXi8TK

For years @telegram has lived in the shadow of other messaging apps.

February 2014 marked @WhatsApp and @Viber as the great winners of that time, gaining a huge financial fortune. https://t.co/3SWJsSxJL3

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber Seven years later, another member of the instant messaging apps family @signalapp, earned a somewhat aristocratic reputation by becoming the new beloved child of some famous tech figures like @elonmusk and @dhh

https://t.co/51Q8HqIItj

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin I think that sense of staleness also resonates with the startup world.

It feels like once a startup overcomes its labor pains, more often than not it becomes stale: social accounts become silenced, and product development seems to be stopped.

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin If I had to draw a timeline of tech hypes in recent years, it would probably look like:

2023 → AI
2017 → Crypto
2016 → Chatbots

At that time, beloved apps of the design community, like Operator and @Lemonade_inc, had already bet on the premise of chatbots.
@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin @Lemonade_Inc Shoutout to @isiluzum for the amazing nostalgic prototype! https://t.co/lbLhBPCVpt

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin @Lemonade_Inc @isiluzum And chatbots have been at the core of Telegram since it was launched. It evolved from simple conversational interactions, and payment integrations to rich Mini Apps. https://t.co/OMnzOy8jcK

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin @Lemonade_Inc @isiluzum One of the things I believe makes a brand strong and memorable is originality.

It might sounds like a contradiction but from its unique illustrations style, colorful visuals and controversial privacy policy — that's what makes Telegram's a real brand. https://t.co/q3e5IgnMVI

@telegram @WhatsApp @Viber @signalapp @elonmusk @dhh @karrisaarinen @brian_lovin @Lemonade_Inc @isiluzum Read the full post on Product Identity

It seems there’s a psychological effect and a sense of attachment to any type of business model:

The villain is the ads model. The Facebooks of our time have basically made it a bad name. I remember @mwseibel said once: "I was embarrassed… https://t.co/VdSO6CrnXf

I view a narrative of business models.

It seems there’s a psychological effect and a sense of attachment to any type of business model:

The villain is the ads model. The Facebooks of our time have basically made it a bad name. I remember @mwseibel said once: "I was embarrassed to say https://t.co/vjdQp9KJPX would monetize with advertising when clearly that was the only answer. Own the simple business model."

The archetype is the subscription model. Although being one of the most popular business models, it tends to trigger people. The inflation in subscription-based apps has largely made people to think more than twice before they insert their credit card and commit to another product or service.

And now the rebel is the lifetime, novel deal. @37signals guys are being applauded after launching ONCE, perhaps rightfully. And I see other successes in the Indie Hackers community with this kind of pricing plan.

I think people are fed up with ads and subscriptions, but I wonder how one-time/lifetime plans will hold.
(cc @damengchen)

I’m not titled as an author but this resonates with me: "“But my writing process is haphazard and scattered.” by @robkhenderson 

It’s amusing how the process evolves. An early draft version begins with brain-dumped bullet points, half-baked paragraphs, and blocks of text scattered around the page.

Then gradually it becomes a somewhat read-to-read document, and being edited from time to time.

“This is not good” and “Do I really make a point here?” thoughts often come and go, but eventually it all aligns and forms into a read-to-publish thing.

In this world of distraction, @AREdotNA presence is bliss.

A lot has been discussed, produced, and filmed around the culture of mainstream social networks. We've become a society addicted to distraction, chasing after high dopamine doses.

Manipulated by platform interests and VC money, we're attached to screens. Our minds spin around ads, and 'For you' tabs aiming for infinite feed scrolling.

So when culture and industries revolve around the same ideas, it's worth highlighting others choosing the opposite path.

Many know https://t.co/TqIokjxTu2 yet it still goes under the radar in many circles. Unlike other tech companies, it doesn't make a regular appearance on mainstream channels.

Born from the ashes of https://t.co/wzLEhqtcON, https://t.co/TqIokjxTu2 isn't backed by any VC but by its modest community. As a kind of social platform, ads don't exist there.

But it plays a big part in my creative process—filled with bookmarks of visuals, links, and highlighted texts. It is like a shared land where people tend alone or together some of the niched-weird yet exciting digital gardens.

Thanks to the lack of typical ‘getting famous’ metrics and an aggressive algorithm https://t.co/nslCQReCBy just feels like a calm place on the noisy internet. It seems like it's driven by authenticity and uniqueness.  Maybe this is what a social antidote feels like.

What perhaps interests me the most since @elonmusk takeover is how Twitter will evolve as a text medium.

Besides new additions like the X semi-rebranding, Revshare, the algo(?) changes, or renaming the tweet terminology, I think the most crucial thing was removing the 280 character limit.

I don't know if removing the characters limit was inevitable, threading was going great but it changed something in this place's identity. It might not bother people much (the rules are determined by the platform), but squeezing thoughts into short-form tweets was largely part of the magic.

Some important points.

I'm afraid AI is going to degenerate us. It actually already started to.

It's summarizing articles for us.
It's generating tweets and posts for us.
It's browsing for us.

Taking the “hassle” out and automate it can be great for some grunt work, but creativity required far more engagement with oneself. Skipping part of the process by delegating it to a machine hurt the the process imo.

I think tools should enablers rather than being fully self-sufficient.
When I search with @ExaAILabs and @MarginaliaNu I get results, not "complete summaries". Also they prioritize quality over SEO quantity.

It would take far more time to rediscovering the internet because there were no good tools for doing that. Now it becomes much more accessible.

I realized how deep I am in the tech bubble after I started writing a publication.

I thought I'd be writing in many different fields, but then I discovered most of my experience, and thus my ideas relate to software and other tech-geeky stuff.

There's nothing wrong about it. But it does lead me to think about how to be involved in design aspects other than tech.

I wrote about designing for a single purpose, or in other words, designing for longevity.

A few bits behind the scenes:

https://t.co/5PsvGeeqkK

A few months ago I saw Jeff's tweet, which I really resonated with. Maybe because I felt it was answering a question I asked myself for a long time: "how should I take photos?"

I kept thinking about it.

https://t.co/Sfb4Y4wUOf

Another few months later @sariazout posted this moodboard, and something immediately clicked.

I started to noticed a pattern. The single-purpose mindset seeks a real purpose rather than just growth and money:

https://t.co/KUvBSkepv2

• You can only read e-books with a Kindle.
• There’s no app store on the @thelightphone.
• You’ll mostly find waterproof apparel in a Rains store.
• The design of @AREdotNA remained the same for more than 12 years.

But sadly, this sense of longevity isn't the norm in VCware. The opposite is true.

We’ve been taught to go big or go home—raise millions in funding, build a big company, hire more and more employees, and hope for the desired exit.
https://t.co/jxex0XM6GS

Using a product that was designed for a single purpose brings back joy. Design feels timeless.

I was reminded by Eli of the novelty of the iPod Classic slider.

https://t.co/lwHAHuVuWI

Passiveness and distraction often occur when using a multi-purpose product.

And perhaps going back to basics is inevitable. When reading on my Kindle I don’t get bothered by messages or incoming calls. I’m just focused on the act of reading.

https://t.co/7zQ1vLpfAA
Special thanks to @k7vin for reading a draft and making sure I'm getting somewhere.

In light of the recent AI hardware discourse, I'm trying to get my head around the case for new hardware. Currently thinking about three segments:

1. Reinventing: creating new paradigms, replacing existing models of ways to consume or create. e.g. The Car, TV, Smartphone, etc.

2. Improving: packaging a known experience to a familiar shape. e.g. Smart Watch, Bluetooth earphones, Google Home, Amazon Alexa

3. Entertaining: creating new experiences purposely to entertain, often while creating a new culture.
e.g. Tamagotchi, Game Boy

Looking at hardware design in the wearable AI parade gives me the impression that people are inclined to reinvent an artifact's shape because it suggests "new capabilities".
https://t.co/X1kTj2Z2rd

I don't know what the future holds, still, I'm not convinced AI is the next enabler for "next-gen" hardware.

- Do we want a new type of artifact to carry?
- Does AI more of a software layer than hardware?

A disclaimer: I've tried zero wearable AI, but from an outsider's perspective the Smart Glasses look like a solid take. It's simply repackaged in a known form.

- No new hardware to develop
- Integrates with an everyday artifact (and a basic need)

What’s inside the dark forests of the internet?

I explored the cozy web and more obscure dark forests: email and comment sections.

Email is no longer considered as a social gathering space. However, I recently discovered Bits and Bobs, published by @komorama. It's a public gdoc where subscribers comment and discuss with each other, detached from any feed and engagement metrics.
https://t.co/8hN0R84jGi

Comment sections on public social and news media websites are arguably the most disreputable corners of these platforms. Some major outlets like @VICE and @npr have already closed their own almost a decade ago.

But as it turns out, applying the same dynamics to smaller, cozier spaces has the opposite effect of large-scale platforms—it cultivates meaningful discussions. Evidence can be found in small blogs like those of @erikphoel, @om and many other @SubstackInc publications.

Maybe there’s an infrastructure waiting to emerge—something that could make dark forests a little less dark.
https://t.co/dw9gHWQZM5

Tracing the niche design idea back to its grassroots.

It's been a few years in the making including many iterations.

The first one was probably started in this ranting thread on the state of the design community
https://t.co/J1I6neJiZ1

The 2nd was quietly developed into a more opinionated version and published on @futurelandtv after sitting in my drafts folder for a few months.

I was inspired by weird products like @AREdotNA, @KinopioClub, @NotionHQ, @MuseAppHQ, and @RoamResearch
https://t.co/THmC4aEPZd

A 3rd iteration was a mini-tweetstorm, which was highly influenced by @pieratt 'notes on slowness' and @asallen 'no more boring apps'
https://t.co/V2vlvlHvYq

Then came the 4th iteration, a blog post on product identity that was built on the previous thread
https://t.co/bLWyh1QQxH

Now, the 5th iteration is the most ambitious yet—taking the form of a zine and directly incorporating the voices that first inspired the idea
https://t.co/0kgQnP9dhg

"building as gardening" is a perspective I've heard over and over while talking to some niche builders

in the build-fast-break-things-pivot-again culture, I think that's a healthier way to make stuff; you don't reinforce a solution but rather let it grow more organically

Cab first mentioned this metaphor while discussing how @AREdotNA balances between speed and progress

then convincing someone to use your product doesn't feel artificial https://t.co/KAnkvhm2S0

then Herman of https://t.co/fKNxLg2fZF articulated it in a way that speaks for itself

instead of treating product development as construction with countless plans and goals to meet, you can treat it more like a garden—with countless unknowns and possibilities a thing can grow https://t.co/6YXquzWZSH

I've been pondering this question, and honestly, observing my experience, I think the best thing one could do is to find a community, and not necessarily a design one.
https://t.co/uV7Qi1mCul

for example when I was into torrents in my youth, I got involved with the community. over time I've helped others to launch their own websites, while helping with design.

so I spent less time on the learning act itself, and was actually designing for a real purpose.

I've always found the design community to be the best resource. be it dribbble, designernews or deviantart of the past, or @AREdotNA of these days, which btw is the only place I would point design newcomers.

though I've read a few books and traditional literature, I can't say any of them really changed my perspective.

I'd also stay away from tooling courses and similar learning materials. such places tend to be more about tool praising rather on the design process itself.

part of the reason I’m making the niche design zine is due to the lack of communities and in-depth resources in this space
https://t.co/0kgQnP9dhg
I guess the caveat here is that I've got no formal design education, so perhaps I can speak only for capable, autodidactic people.

After more than a year in the making, the Niche design magazine is officially out.

Through essays and interviews with designers and builders, it explores where design flourishes in this age of sameness.

A sewn-bound paperback will begin shipping soon.

https://t.co/sonawOAMnK https://t.co/SFvqxGUdpY

As a contrast to the conventional tech-design playbook where clout is measured in fully booked calendars, hallucinated funding rounds, and inflated valuations, Niche design proposes a different ethos—one that’s calm, intentional, and grounded in care. It asks not how big we might…

In conversation w/: @broskoski @AREdotNA @repponen @pavelkedich @davegorum

& words from @pketh @asallen @notboring @thecpe @wade @usbclub @internetvin @lucaslovexoxo @kentdebruin @daylightco @xhfloz @thelightphone @teenageengin33r @sariazout https://t.co/4eRnVHfDYT

As a contrast to the conventional tech-design playbook where clout is measured in fully booked calendars, hallucinated funding rounds, and inflated valuations, Niche design proposes a different ethos—one that’s calm, intentional, and grounded in care. It asks not how big we might scale, but how deep we might go.

Thank you all for participating in this long project and for tolerating my ongoing nudges and requests.

Special thanks to the 70+ people who backed this project when it was just a public Google Doc. Your support, both mentally and financially, made this real.

Feeling like sharing a project from ~2 years ago that was called ThoughtCast

The idea was to record a thought and get it refined to be shared as mini-podcasts https://t.co/L5OB57elsZ
A voice recording would be turned into a 60-90 clip, cloned in your own voice with a cool background wallpaper https://t.co/QoU36VBwDh
Too bad I can't find more live recordings in the archive but for a few weeks people were having fun with this thing https://t.co/l1bUK0NJw9

At the time we were studying the history of social audio, like @mignano's Anchor and @rrhoover audio blogs. And also Airchat started blooming, so it seemed like a cool concept for a while https://t.co/8Yhpn5Tg44
I worked on this project together with @OmryRozenfeld, experimenting with a few AI toolings, like @elevenlabsio for voice cloning, and @StabilityAI DreamStudio for image generation

This resonates with me so much. There's this notion of "do your thing and they'll come", and while that's true to some extent, from what I've been experiencing, it's not enough.

I realized being super-proactive is inevitable: approach people, send cold emails, promote your thing https://t.co/69WZ5BaSQn https://t.co/veK61t5DFw
It's hard, daunting, and time-consuming, but nobody will promote your things but you. Being not-chosen-yet is very frustrating, but sometimes you can flip the situation and make them choose you.
Want to participate in a podcast? Want to get featured somewhere? Want to be interviewed? Do it yourself rather than waiting for someone to discover you.

Communities should serve as a fertile ground for interaction, not only engagement in its shallow meaning, i.e., thumbs up, likes, or emoji spreading. https://t.co/a89oI1LJHH

I'd like to propose another level, and perhaps more deep: long-form prints/reports.

Niche design started as a tweet, then became a Sustack post, and eventually turned into a ~100 zine. https://t.co/ZjrKBAnItq

Substack is great for high-effort writing, but publishing a printed matter, or even gathering a bunch of essays into a cohesive PDF feels more formal on one hand, but it's also the culmination of my essays and thoughts. Nothing has ever felt more fulfilling to me.
It's like dedicating a few years to writing a book, and once you finish, the outcome is like publishing a PhD. (excuse my analogy, I've never done that)

a framing I liked by @karoljmajewski is that Niche design can be treated simply as design research. digital design is relatively young, but mature enough to observe.

I used to think of research as being in more abstract topics where the act of study is more theoretical. 

having done the first issue, I can see that this is more of a practical research, yet it goes through many other perspectives, and cultural phenomena
interestingly, there aren't many established design research, other than @johnmaeda and other pop-up reports

@karoljmajewski also thinking of it as pure research brought into discussion the opportunity to fund it through a grant, or other funding vehicles

I couldn't really think of it in that way before, but now it might seem feasible

It was printed in A.R print in Tel Aviv, which is like an institution here, but I’m not sure they work outside the country (I can’t find if they even has an English website version). it's quite surprising that crypto as a crowdfund mechanism hasn't matured out of the hype

it was much more lucrative to fund work via tokens back in 2021, but it was probably more of speculation other than anything (though people have gotten real support and funds)

I'm now considering a crowdfund for the next niche design issue, and I really lean into the idea of making it through crypto

@carlbarenbrug suggested me to make a do a kickstarter, but since it only supports stripe + the ease of creating my own platform is easier, it feels more…
I don't want to initiate any token or promising fluff, just enabling people to support and fund my work and the magazine in more ways which might yield other interesting outcomes

I'm now considering a crowdfund for the next niche design issue, and I really lean into the idea of making it through crypto
